one_line_takeaway: TrojanEdit shows that naively combining text+image triggers in backdooring multimodal diffusion image editors collapses to a unimodal backdoor due to gradient-dominance ('modality bias'), and its BKG gradient-rebalancing method fixes this to reach ~97-98% ASR with near-zero false-trigger rate while evading image-compression, Fine-pruning, and TIJO defenses.
key_finding: Across the four target models, TrojanEdit's multimodal trigger achieves an average ASR of 97.75% (image attack) and 96.00% (style attack) while keeping average EAR at 0.25% (image attack) and 1.25% (style attack) -- the lowest EAR among all compared methods, essentially matching visual-only baselines' low false-trigger rate while matching or exceeding textual baselines' high ASR. Average CLIP-I similarity for TrojanEdit is 81.25% (image) and 96.00% (style), comparable to or better than most baselines, indicating minimal degradation of normal editing quality. The ablation (Table 5, on InstructPix2Pix) shows that WITHOUT BKG balance learning, the textual trigger alone still activates the backdoor (96.35% ASR) while the visual trigger alone does not (0% ASR) -- confirming the predicted modality-bias collapse; WITH BKG, ASR is 98.83% only when both triggers are present and near 0% for either trigger alone, confirming the balanced multimodal backdoor requires both modalities jointly. Under robustness testing, TrojanEdit retains ASR above 85% even at 60% JPEG-quality compression and at Fine-pruning retention down to 10%, and TIJO's detection AUC stays low across all four models and all trigger-modality settings (e.g., multimodal-modality AUC ranges 18.73-41.24%, near or below chance for a detector), indicating none of the three tested defenses reliably detects or mitigates the attack.
core_contribution: The paper presents TrojanEdit, the first study of backdoor attacks against multimodal (text+image conditioned) diffusion-based image editing models. It identifies a 'backdoor modality bias' phenomenon -- naively combining textual and visual triggers causes the model to learn only the modality with the stronger gradient signal (typically the textual trigger), collapsing to a de facto unimodal backdoor and degrading normal editing quality. The authors give a theoretical analysis (Theorem 1, under stated conditions C1-C3) showing that if one modality's gradient norm asymptotically dominates the other's by a factor gamma>1, the weaker modality's cumulative parameter update vanishes in the limit, driving the model toward unimodal reliance. To fix this they propose BKG (Backdoor Gradients) Multimodal Balance Learning, which computes per-step gradients from text-only-triggered and visual-only-triggered variants of a poisoned sample, forms a discrepancy ratio rho = (||G_text||+eps)/(||G_vis||+eps), and rescales G_text by 1/rho and G_vis by rho before summing into a single balanced update -- forcing the model to require BOTH triggers simultaneously to activate the backdoor.
threat_model: Attacker is a malicious third-party model provider who releases a pretrained/fine-tuned image editing model for public download (a supply-chain/model-provider threat model, consistent with prior diffusion backdoor work). The attacker has full access to training data and the training process (can poison samples and control fine-tuning). Attack goals: on clean (prompt, image) inputs the model edits normally; on inputs carrying both a textual trigger (the character '!' appended to the prompt) and a visual trigger (a 16x16 white patch on the input image), the model instead outputs an attacker-chosen target -- either a fixed target image (a 'cat' image and a 'girl' photo were used as targets) or a fixed target style ('black and white').
limitations: The paper's own scope limits it acknowledges: (1) trigger design is simple/fixed (a single white 16x16 patch and a single '!' character) rather than exploring semantically-aligned or stealthier cross-modal triggers, left as future work; (2) only three defenses are tested (image compression, Fine-pruning, TIJO) and no adaptive/multimodal-aware defense specifically designed against BKG-style balanced backdoors is evaluated; (3) transferability of the multimodal backdoor across different model architectures or datasets is explicitly named as unstudied and left to future work; (4) the theoretical analysis (Theorem 1) is an asymptotic/idealized argument (assumes orthogonal per-modality gradients and a persistent gradient-norm ratio condition C3) rather than a finite-sample guarantee, and its conditions (C1-C4, though only C1-C3 are stated in the visible text) are not empirically verified to hold throughout real training; (5) evaluation relies on a ResNet-50 classifier (not human judgment) to score ASR/EAR, introducing potential classifier-error dependence, though the paper reports >90% classifier accuracy.

datasets_used: LAION-400M subset (CLIP-filtered 400M image-text pairs), split 1:9 train:test
quantitative_results: TrojanEdit average ASR (image attack) = 97.75%, average EAR = 0.25% (Table 2)
TrojanEdit average ASR (style attack) = 96.00%, average EAR = 1.25% (Table 3)
TrojanEdit average CLIP-I similarity = 81.25% (image attack), 96.00% (style attack) (Table 4)
Ablation on InstructPix2Pix (Table 5): without BKG, textual-trigger-only ASR = 96.35%, visual-trigger-only ASR = 0%; with BKG, multimodal-trigger ASR = 98.83%, single-modality-only ASR ~0%
Poisoning rate fixed at 4% for main experiments; ASR remains high even at 2% poisoning rate, with ASR increasing and CLIP-I decreasing as poisoning rate rises to 10% (Fig. 8)
Robustness: ASR remains above 85% under image compression down to 60% quality (Fig. 9) and under Fine-pruning down to 10% retention (Fig. 10)
TIJO detection AUC against TrojanEdit ranges from 11.06% to 41.24% across four models and three trigger-modality conditions (Table 6), indicating poor detection performance
ResNet-50 classifier used to score ASR/EAR achieved >90% accuracy recognizing backdoor target images during testing
